title: The Team
text:
The people behind HealthScope
  



  

    
      CM
      Christina Mallya
      Data Scientist
      
        Leads the research design, data extraction pipeline, and analytical framework for HealthScope. Specializes in health facility data harmonization, geospatial analysis, and malaria epidemiology across Sub-Saharan Africa.
      
    

    
      KC
      Kasim Chambulilo
      Data Scientist
      
        Builds and maintains the HealthScope portal, ETL pipelines, and interactive data visualizations. Develops the open-source infrastructure for data processing, quality assessment, and web-based facility mapping.

title: 🇰🇪 Kenya
text: Republic of Kenya. Health Facility Dataset v1.1.

title: 🇰🇪 Kenya
text:
Overview
Kenya operates one of the most comprehensive open health facility registries in sub-Saharan Africa — the Kenya Master Health Facility List (KMHFL). The Portal dataset is derived from KMHFL quarterly extracts, supplemented with DHIS2 codes and coordinate cross-validation across all 47 counties.

title: 🇰🇪 Kenya
text:
Code Example
library(hfportal)

ken <- get_facilities(country = "KEN", version = "1.1")

library(dplyr)
ken |>
  count(admin1, facility_type) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(admin1, n), y = n, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_col() + coord_flip() + theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Kenya: Facilities by County & Type", x = NULL)
Dataset last updated: August 2024 | Source: KMHFL (Kenya MoH)

title: Help & Support
text:
Find answers to common questions about the data and the portal
  




  Frequently Asked Questions

  

  
    
      How do I access the health facilities data?
      ▼
    
    
      Navigate to the Country Data page and select your country of interest.
      You can explore the interactive map to view facility locations and then download the full dataset in CSV format.
      All data is openly downloadable.
    
  

  
    
      How do I find a nearby health facility?
      ▼
    
    
      Use our Find Facility tool. Allow your browser to detect your location (or enter coordinates manually),
      select the service type you need, and the system will show you the nearest facilities offering that service on an interactive map.
    
  

  
    
      What file formats are available for download?
      ▼
    
    
      Data is provided as CSV — standard comma-separated values for spreadsheet and statistical analysis.
    
  


  
    
      How frequently is the data updated?
      ▼
    
    
      Core facility lists are updated quarterly when new data sources become available. Service availability data
      is refreshed when Ministries of Health publish new registry exports. Coordinate validation is ongoing. Each dataset release includes
      a version number and changelog so you can track what has changed.
    
  

  
    
      What countries are currently covered?
      ▼
    
    
      Coverage is expanding across Sub-Saharan Africa. Visit the
      Registry Explorer page to see which countries are currently available.
    
  

  
    
      How do I report data quality issues or errors?
      ▼
    
    
      If you discover errors or inconsistencies in the data, we appreciate feedback!
      Email the DET Team
      with the country, facility name, and a description of the issue.
    
  




  Email Contact
  Prefer direct email? Reach out to our team:
  
    DET Team
  
  We typically respond within 2–3 business days.




 Back to top

title: Data Sources
text:
Authoritative sources powering our health facilities database
  




  Primary Data Sources
  We collate facility data from multiple authoritative sources and validate against cross-references

  

    
    
      
        
          📋
        
      
      National Registries
      Master facility lists maintained by Ministries of Health across East Africa. Official authoritative source for facility licensing and accreditation.
      
        Point Data
        Official
        Licensed
      
      Visit Source
    

    
    
      
        
          🗺️
        
      
      OpenStreetMap
      Community-contributed health facility data from OpenStreetMap. Used for cross-reference validation and coverage assessment in underreported regions.
      
        Point Data
        Community
        ODbL
      
      Visit Source
    

  




  Data Update Frequency
  Our datasets follow a structured update schedule to ensure accuracy and timeliness:
  
    Core Facility Lists: Updated quarterly or when new data sources become available
    Service Availability: Refreshed when MoH registries publish new exports
    Coordinate Validation: Continuously verified against reference datasets
    Quality Reports: Regenerated with each dataset version release
  




  Data Harmonization Methodology
  
    All source datasets are harmonized to a standard schema capturing: unique facility ID, official name, administrative hierarchy
    (country/region/district), facility type (hospital/clinic/health center), ownership (public/private/NGO), GPS coordinates,
    bed capacity, operational status, last verification date, and service availability indicators. Quality assessment includes
    duplicate detection, coordinate validation, admin hierarchy consistency, and temporal completeness checks.
  




 Back to top
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Zambia has approximately 3,000 health facilities across its ten provinces, governed by the Ministry of Health through district health offices. The country has undergone significant health system reforms, including the rollout of community health workers and the integration of HIV and malaria services. Urban–rural disparities remain a key challenge, with Lusaka province having significantly better facility density than rural provinces like Western and North-Western.

Demographics
Zambia’s population of around 20 million is served by a tiered system of health posts, health centres, district hospitals, and tertiary facilities. Health posts are designed to serve approximately 500–1,000 households in rural areas. The country has a physician density of approximately 1.2 per 10,000 population, with nurses and midwives forming the majority of the health workforce.

Services
Zambian health facilities provide malaria diagnosis and treatment (including ACT and IPTp for pregnant women), HIV testing and ART, PMTCT, maternal and child health, immunization, family planning, TB services, and management of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) at higher-level facilities. Malaria transmission is perennial in most provinces, with higher intensity in the rainy season (November–April).

Data Download
Download facilities dataset here:

Facilities — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Tanzania has one of the largest health facility networks in sub-Saharan Africa, with over 8,000 facilities spread across 26 regions. The health system is organized into a tiered structure ranging from dispensaries at the village level to national referral hospitals. Despite significant progress in expanding access, rural and remote areas still face challenges with staffing, supply chains, and infrastructure.

Demographics
Tanzania’s health facilities primarily serve a population of approximately 63 million people, with a high proportion residing in rural areas. Dispensaries account for the majority of facilities and typically serve catchment populations of 6,000–10,000 people. Health centres serve larger populations and are expected to cover around 50,000 people each. The country faces a critical shortage of health workers, with an estimated 1.7 physicians per 10,000 population.

Services
Tanzanian health facilities provide outpatient consultations, maternal and child health (MCH) services, malaria diagnosis and treatment, HIV/AIDS testing and antiretroviral therapy (ART), tuberculosis (TB) diagnosis and treatment, family planning, and immunization. Malaria remains a leading cause of outpatient visits, particularly in high-transmission zones along the coast and Lake Victoria basin.

Data Download
Download the facilities data here:

Facilities — CSV
Services — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Malawi operates a mixed health system with government facilities managed by the Ministry of Health and a significant proportion run by the Christian Health Association of Malawi (CHAM), which provides nearly 40% of health services. The country has around 1,000 health facilities across its 28 districts. Despite being a low-income country, Malawi has achieved notable gains in reducing child and maternal mortality over recent decades.

Demographics
Malawi has a population of approximately 20 million, with over 80% living in rural areas. Most facilities are health centres and dispensaries serving rural communities. The country faces severe health workforce shortages, with one of the lowest doctor-to-population ratios in the world approximately 0.2 physicians per 10,000 people. Community health workers (Health Surveillance Assistants) play a critical role in bridging service gaps.

Services
Malawian health facilities deliver outpatient care, malaria prevention (including insecticide-treated nets distribution), diagnosis and treatment, maternal and neonatal health, HIV/AIDS services including ART and PMTCT, TB treatment, immunization, and nutrition services. Malaria, respiratory infections, and diarrhoeal diseases are the most common reasons for facility visits.

Data Download
Download facilities dataset here:

Facilities — CSV
Services — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Botswana has one of the more developed health systems in sub-Saharan Africa, supported by the country’s relative economic prosperity from diamond revenues. The country has around 1,000 health facilities including clinics, primary hospitals, district hospitals, and referral hospitals. The government provides largely free primary health care to citizens, and the country has achieved remarkable success in its HIV response.

Demographics
With a population of approximately 2.5 million and a relatively low population density, Botswana’s health facilities serve both urban centres and widely dispersed rural communities. The country has a higher physician density than most African peers, at around 3–4 per 10,000 population. Mobile health facilities and outreach teams support populations in remote districts such as the Central Kalahari.

Services
Botswana’s facilities offer comprehensive primary care, one of Africa’s most successful HIV/ART programmes (with over 85% of eligible patients on treatment), TB services, maternal and child health, immunization, malaria diagnosis and treatment (primarily in the northern districts bordering Zimbabwe and Zambia), mental health services, and management of NCDs including diabetes and hypertension.
Service availability has been extracted from the Botswana Master Facility List and standardised through the HealthScope ETL pipeline into the same 3-level domain hierarchy used for every other country.

Data Download
Download Botswana datasets here:

Facilities — CSV
Services — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.

title: About HealthScope
text: Health facility intelligence beyond static inventories.

title: About HealthScope
text:
Mission
HealthScope is an open-access health facility intelligence portal designed to move beyond static facility inventories toward dynamic, demand-driven health access analytics across malaria-endemic countries in Sub-Saharan Africa. Developed from data extracted directly from Ministries of Health, HealthScope transforms raw registry data into actionable intelligence.
The portal reveals not just where health facilities exist, but whether populations can realistically access them and what services they offer.

title: About HealthScope
text:
The Gap We Fill
Current approaches to health facility mapping focus primarily on documenting facility locations without deeper analysis. HealthScope goes further by providing:

Population-demand modelling – reachability analysis beyond simple location pins
Service-level intelligence – mapping of what services exist where
General information at scale – population-weighted access metrics and facility distribution analysis

HealthScope addresses these gaps as an open, reproducible data platform.

title: About HealthScope
text:
Intelligence Layers


  
    1. Facility Registry Lake
    Standardized facility data from MoH sources across SSA countries. Multi-country harmonized schema; direct MoH sourcing.
  

  
    2. Demand-Side Access
    Population catchment zones using road networks; travel-time isochrones showing not just location, but reachability.
  

  
    3. Service Gap Intelligence
    Which services exist where. Per-domain coverage across the registry, interactive country switching, and per-facility service profiles.
  

  
    4. General Information
    Country-level descriptive statistics — facility counts, type mix, ownership split — that give a fast picture of the registry without claiming reachability or access modelling we have not yet built.

title: About HealthScope
text:
ETL Pipeline
The HealthScope ETL pipeline ingests each Ministry of Health registry through three steps:

Extract — pull the latest country CSV / API payload (Tanzania HFR portal, Kenya KMHFR, Ethiopia MFR v2, etc.) into a country-specific raw folder.
Transform — standardise columns to a shared schema, validate coordinates against the country’s bounding box, mint a stable global facility UID, and map each raw service name to the canonical 7-domain hierarchy via per-country crosswalks.
Load — publish versioned per-country CSVs ({country}_standardized.csv for facilities, {country}_services_standardized.csv for services) that the portal consumes directly via a git submodule.


Current Pipeline Status

title: About HealthScope
text:
Data Sources
HealthScope ingests only authoritative, official sources — Ministry of Health master facility registries and openly-licensed compilations of the same upstream data. Each country’s row in the pipeline status above links back to its primary registry. For the full source catalogue with license and citation details, visit the dedicated page.
View Data Sources →

title: About HealthScope
text:
Team
The portal is maintained by a team of data engineers, GIS specialists, epidemiologists, and researchers at the Malaria Atlas Project Dar es Salaam Node.
Visit the Team Page →
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Benin’s health system is structured into three levels: peripheral (health zones), intermediate (departmental), and central (national). The country has around 900 public health facilities complemented by a growing private sector. Health zone offices coordinate service delivery across Benin’s 12 departments. Despite improvements, access to care remains unequal, with the northern regions facing greater barriers due to distance and infrastructure.

Demographics
Benin has a population of approximately 13 million, with high fertility and a young population structure. Most public facilities are community health centres (CSC/CCS) serving rural communities. The country has a significant shortage of skilled health personnel, particularly in rural zones, with a physician density of approximately 0.7 per 10,000 population. Community health agents (relais communautaires) extend the reach of facility-based care.

Services
Health facilities in Benin provide outpatient consultations, antenatal care, malaria prevention (IPTp, ITN distribution) and treatment, HIV testing and ART, immunization under the Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI), nutrition services, and family planning. Malaria is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in Benin, particularly in children under five, with high transmission in the south.

Data Download
Download the facilities dataset here:

CSV

RDS
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Ethiopia operates one of the largest health systems in sub-Saharan Africa, organised in a three-tier structure: primary health care (health posts, health centres and primary hospitals), general hospitals, and specialised / tertiary hospitals. The Federal Ministry of Health maintains the centralised Master Facility Registry v2, and this dataset reflects a recent CSV export from that registry covering all 11 regional administrations and the two chartered city administrations.

Demographics
With a population of approximately 125 million — the second-largest in Africa — Ethiopia faces enormous service-delivery demand. The Health Extension Programme has placed two trained extension workers at each rural kebele-level health post, making the country a continental reference for community-based primary care. Doctor density remains low (~1.1 per 10,000), with nurses, midwives and Health Extension Workers carrying most of the front-line workload.

Services
MFR v2 records four service domains per facility — clinical, diagnostic, support, and 24-hour services — which the HealthScope ETL pipeline unnests and maps to the standard 3-level hierarchy used for every other country. The public MFR v2 CSV export does not include GPS, so coordinates are joined from the openly-licensed WHO AFRO compilation on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (~63% direct match by Facility ID); facilities without an HDX coordinate (mostly drug stores and small private clinics outside WHO AFRO’s traditional coverage scope) are excluded from the map.

Data Download
Download Ethiopia datasets here:

Facilities — CSV
Services — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline from the Ethiopia MoH Master Facility Registry v2, with coordinates joined from the WHO AFRO compilation on HDX.
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Nigeria has the largest health facility network in Africa, with over 40,000 facilities ranging from primary health care centres (PHCs) to federal tertiary hospitals. The health system is decentralized across federal, state, and local government levels, which creates significant variability in service quality and availability across the country’s 36 states and FCT. Northern states generally have lower facility density and worse health outcomes compared to southern states.

Data Download
Download the facilities datset here:

Facilities — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.
Standardized, geolocated facility information to strengthen modelling, planning, and decision-making
Health Facilities Map

Overview
Uganda’s health system comprises approximately 6,000 government and private facilities, structured into a six-tier system from Village Health Teams (VHTs) at the community level to national referral hospitals. The Ministry of Health has made significant efforts to decentralize health services, though inequities persist between urban and rural areas, particularly in the northern regions.

Demographics
With a population of around 47 million — one of the fastest-growing in the world — Uganda’s health facilities face significant demand pressure. Health centre IIs and IIIs form the backbone of primary care, each serving populations of approximately 5,000 and 20,000 respectively. Uganda has a physician density of roughly 0.5 per 10,000 population, and relies heavily on nurses, midwives, and community health workers to deliver frontline care.

Services
Ugandan facilities offer outpatient and inpatient care, malaria diagnosis (RDT and microscopy) and treatment, PMTCT (prevention of mother-to-child transmission of HIV), ART, immunization, reproductive and maternal health services, nutrition support, and TB management. Malaria is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality, accounting for a large share of all outpatient visits, especially in children under five.

Data Download
Download the facilities data here:

Facilities — CSV

Produced by the HealthScope ETL pipeline.

title: Methodology
text: How we source, clean, harmonise, and quality-assess health facility data

title: Methodology
text:
Overview
This page describes the end-to-end pipeline used to produce each country dataset. The pipeline runs through four stages: source collation → harmonization → quality assessment → versioned release. All pipeline code is open-source and available in the GitHub repository.

title: Methodology
text:
1. Source Collation

Primary Sources



Country
Primary Source
Update Frequency




Tanzania
MOH Tanzania
Annual


Uganda
MoH Uganda HMIS
Annual





Secondary Sources
Where the MFL has coverage gaps we supplement with DHIS2 organisation unit exports, OpenStreetMap for coordinate cross-validation, and HDX historical snapshots (e.g. the WHO AFRO Ethiopia coordinate compilation).

title: Methodology
text:
2. Harmonization

Common Schema
All datasets are transformed to a shared flat schema before analysis:
facility_id     character   Portal-assigned unique ID (ISO2_NNNNN)
source_id       character   Identifier in the original source
name            character   Facility name (original language)
facility_type   character   Hospital / Health Centre / Dispensary / Clinic / Other
ownership       character   Public / Private-for-profit / Faith-based / NGO
admin1          character   Province / Region
admin2          character   District / County
admin3          character   Division / Sub-county (if available)
admin4          character   Ward / Parish (if available)
longitude       numeric     WGS84 decimal degrees
latitude        numeric     WGS84 decimal degrees
coord_source    character   GPS / Geocoded / Centroid / Unknown
operational     logical     Operational at time of data extraction
year            integer     Reference year of the record
version         character   Portal dataset version

title: Methodology
text:
3. Quality Assessment

  📍 Coordinate Validity
  Proportion of records with non-missing GPS coordinates falling within the
  country boundary. Centroid-imputed coordinates are flagged separately.



  🗂 Attribute Completeness
  Proportion of non-missing values per schema field, reported individually and
  as an overall completeness score (0–100).



  🔁 Duplicate Rate
  Estimated proportion of duplicate records before and after deduplication
  using the name × coordinate matching procedure.



  📅 Temporal Coverage
  Year range spanned by records, and the proportion of facilities with a
  recorded update date within the past 24 months.



  🏛 Administrative Consistency
  Proportion of records whose admin1–admin2 codes are consistent with
  official GADM v4.1 administrative boundary files.

title: Methodology
text:
4. Versioned Release
Datasets follow semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH):

MAJOR — schema-breaking changes
MINOR — new facilities or additional fields (backward-compatible)
PATCH — coordinate corrections or name fixes


About the Project
Roadmap
